description: This dual-Y-axis scatter plot illustrates the trade-off between yield strength (Rp0.2, MPa) and two critical mechanical properties: Charpy impact energy (J/cm², left blue axis) and total elongation (%, right red axis) for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steels and benchmark alloys. The X-axis spans 300–1000 MPa yield strength. Solid blue symbols (circles, triangles, squares) represent the investigated Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W steel processed with different hot-rolling reductions (45%, 68%, 82%), showing a gradual increase in yield strength from ~480 MPa to ~648 MPa with decreasing Charpy energy from ~133 J/cm² to ~105 J/cm² while maintaining high total elongation of 50–60%. Green stars mark specific high-performance states of the studied alloy. Red open symbols denote comparison materials including solution-treated austenitic stainless steels and other Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys, which typically exhibit lower elongation (<40%) and impact toughness at similar or lower strength levels. The data demonstrate that increasing hot-rolling reduction refines grain size and increases dislocation density, achieving significant strengthening without sacrificing the excellent ductility and toughness characteristic of high-manganese austenitic steels, outperforming conventional age-hardened steels which show comparable strength but markedly inferior ductility (~21%) and toughness.
